food_name: Taiwan Home Cook Dry Ramen Noodles

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Taiwan-style dry ramen or mazesoba. FOODON_00002802 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Noodles, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific mazesoba or Taiwan dry ramen code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for mazesoba or Taiwan-style dry ramen as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Taiwan Home Cook dry ramen as served at a ramen restaurant, approximately 350 g, comprising cooked wheat noodles tossed in a savoury sauce with toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Noodles, wheat, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20110) adapted with dry ramen sauce and topping composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base noodle values derived from USDA FoodData Central cooked wheat noodles (FDC ID 169737). Macros adjusted upward to reflect the addition of soy-based tare sauce, sesame oil dressing, and pork chashu toppings typical of Taiwan-style dry ramen as served at ramen restaurants. Energy, fat, protein, and sodium values are estimated composites. This is a medium-confidence adapted estimate; exact restaurant recipe not publicly available.

gi_source.title: Noodles, wheat, boiled — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney (Atkinson et al., 2008)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 is based on boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283). Taiwan-style dry ramen uses the same wheat noodle base; the dry-tossed preparation without broth does not substantially alter the GI relative to boiled wheat noodles. GL per serving calculated as (52 x 96.25 g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 50.1, where serving carbohydrate = 27.5 g per 100g x 350g serving / 100 = 96.25 g. gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-based tare sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of Taiwan-style dry ramen due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (approximately 1680 mg per standard 350 g bowl), which represents a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce or low-sodium sauce options is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 52) with high glycemic load per full serving due to large noodle portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the glycemic index of wheat noodles is moderate-to-low (GI 52), the glycemic load per standard 350 g bowl is high (approximately 50), so individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion size, pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from pork chashu and sesame oil dressing, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of elevated sodium (approximately 1680 mg per bowl) and saturated fat from pork-based toppings makes Taiwan-style dry ramen a food that individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume occasionally and in reduced portions, preferring leaner protein toppings and requesting lighter sauce application.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taiwan Home Cook Dry Ramen Noodles is a Taiwan-inspired dry ramen dish served at Japanese ramen restaurants, notably at Ramen Matsuri in Singapore. Unlike traditional ramen which is served in broth, this dish features cooked wheat noodles that are tossed in a concentrated savoury sauce — typically a soy-based tare blended with sesame oil — and topped with ingredients such as pork chashu, soft-boiled marinated egg, green onions, and sesame seeds. The preparation method involves boiling the noodles until tender, draining them thoroughly, and then coating them in the sauce so that each strand is evenly dressed. The texture is chewy and springy from the wheat noodles, with a rich, umami-forward flavour profile from the soy tare and rendered pork fat. The dish is sometimes referred to as mazesoba or abura soba in the Japanese ramen tradition, though the Taiwan Home Cook variant may incorporate Taiwanese-influenced seasonings. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, carbohydrate-dominant meal with meaningful protein from pork toppings and egg. Sodium content is elevated due to the concentrated sauce, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), though the glycemic load per full serving is high due to the large noodle portion, which is relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional data exists in approved primary sources for this specific restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked wheat noodle data with composite adjustments for sauce and toppings. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles, which is the appropriate analogous food. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for cooked wheat noodles and the composite topping contribution is not reliably quantifiable without a full recipe breakdown. Vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source composite. Vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source composite. Folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source composite. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source composite. Vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source composite. Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source composite. Trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible in wheat noodle and plant-oil based preparations per USDA data. The food_name in the input record was 'Taiwan Home Cook', which is a menu item name rather than a canonical food name; the canonical name has been set to 'Taiwan Home Cook Dry Ramen Noodles' to reflect the dish type accurately. The cuisine tag 'Japanese' from the input is retained as the dish is served at a Japanese ramen restaurant, though the flavour profile is Taiwan-inspired. Ontology codes use closest_parent match types as no exact codes exist for this specific dish. GI category is low (GI 52 is ≤ 55 per the validator threshold rule).
